**Cottonseed** or **cotton seed** refers to the seed of the cotton plant separated from the bulk of the fiber.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The seeds are small and brown in color, sometimes covered in residual white fiber. The flavor is described as nutty and chickpea-like.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Cottonseed is nutritionally dense, but its use as food is complicated by its content of gossypol, a compound toxic to humans.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The seeds can be processed to lower the gossypol content—however, when cooking with cotton seed it is best to use a cultivar bred for low to no gossypol content.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The seeds can also be processed to extract cottonseed oil"), which is refined to remove the toxic gossypol and then used in cooking.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

## Use

[edit | edit source]

To use cottonseed in cooking, it may be soaked and ground to produce a plant milk, analogous to soy milk, almond milk, and others.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Cottonseed is also used to make a Nigerian cottonseed soup and an Indian beverage called paruthi paal.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Cottonseed oil is used like other vegetable oils for frying, dressings, and more.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)
